title: Let & Const vs Var 🔒
description: Why modern developers abandoned the 'var' keyword forever.

slides.title: The Old Days 🦕
ES6 To The Rescue 🦸‍♀️
The Iron Lock 🔒
slides.content:
## The Const Keyword

While `let` can be updated later on (e.g. `let count=0; count=1;`), **`const`** is short for Constant. It locks a variable forever.

```javascript
const birthYear = 1990;

// If you try to change it... BOOM!
birthYear = 1991; // Fatal TypeError!
```

Using `const` makes your code significantly safer. 

If you know a variable's value should absolutely *never* change in your app, use `const` so that another developer can't accidentally overwrite it later!

slides.title: Try It Out 💻
slides.content: Notice how the system crashes instantly because we tried to overwrite a const variable. Protection is active!

quizData.question: Why is `let` heavily preferred over `var` in modern JavaScript?

options.text: let respects Block `{}` Scoping, while var leaks variables globally

quizData.explanation: var ruins the concept of Local Scope. It leaks out of generic blocks and pollutes the global application environment. 'let' perfectly honors the {} brackets it lives in.

slides.title: The Const Exception 🧐
slides.content:
## Mutating Objects

There is one huge catch with `const`. It locks the *reference*, but not the *contents*.

```javascript
const myUser = { name: "Alice" };

myUser = { name: "Bob" }; // ERROR! Reassigning blocked.

// HOWEVER... this perfectly works!!
myUser.name = "Bob"; // Success!
```

You cannot completely reassign a `const` Object or Array to a new =. But you *can* dynamically `.push()` into it or alter its inner properties via dot notation!

quizData.question: If you define `const numbers = [1, 2];`, which of the following actions is completely ALLOWED by JavaScript?
quizData.explanation: const prevents entire variable reassignment (using the = sign). It does NOT prevent internal mutations like Array.push() or Object mutation.
